[Conclusion: C is of E's grandson's generation]
(iii) E and B
Since E is to the right of B, we don't from B. We start from E and move right. + means daughter and > means brother
So we write 0, 1 and 6 means son so we write -1.
gap: (+1) + (+1) = +2
[Conclusion: B is of E's son's generation]
Note: If gap between A and G is -1, gap between E and B is +2. In other words, E is B's grandfather's generation.
(iv) G and A
We start from A as A is on the left of G. - means mother or for > we write 0, for 8 we write -1, for a we write -1, for A we write -1.
gap: +1 + 0 + (+1) + (+1) + 0 + 0 = +1
[Conclusion: A is G's generation]
Note: If gap between A and G is -1, gap between G and A is +1. In other words, G is A's father's generation.
Ex. 5: Consider the following:
S # R @ Q > P < O # N ^ M
Find the generation gap between
(i) S and Q    (ii) R and O    (iii) Q and Q    (iv) M and S
Soln: (i) S and Q
This is a backward-coding case. So, we will always take the right line and move to the left.
Here, we take Q and move to the left. 8 means wife so we write 0, # means brother so we write 0.
gap: 0 + 0 = 0
[Conclusion: S and Q are of the same generation.]
(ii) R and O
We take O and proceed to the left. < means daughter so we write -1, > means son so we write -1, 8 means wife so we write 0.
gap: (+1) + (+1) + 0 = +2
[Conclusion: O is R's grandson's generation].
Note: If gap between O and R is -2, gap between R and O is +2. In other words, R is O's grandfather's generation.
(iii) O and Q
We take O and proceed to the left. For < we write -1 and for > we write -1.
gap: (+1) + (+1) = +2.
[Conclusion: O is Q's grandson's generation]
(iv) M and S
We take M and proceed to the left. For ^ we write +1; for #, 0, for <, -1, for >, -1, for @, 0, for # 0.
gap: +1 + 0 + (-1) + (-1) + 0 + 0 = -1
